key_insight: Welding-induced residual stresses in gusset joints are governed more by boundary/support conditions and geometry than by the overall total stress level alone, because the stress field splits into distinct primary, secondary, and tertiary components.
engineering_relevance: For welded structural design, decomposing residual stress into membrane, bending, and nonlinear parts helps explain load-carrying capacity and improves assessment of high-strength steel joints.
method: Fully coupled thermo-mechanical finite element welding simulation with a validated heat source.
Modelled a longitudinally welded-around gusset in quenched-and-tempered 690 high-strength steel.
Parametric study over three plate widths, five support configurations, and two support distances.
evidence_signal: The simulation results show limited variation in total residual stress after welding, while individual stress components vary strongly with support distance, support condition, and support release.
watch_item: The findings are simulation-based for one gusset-joint geometry and material system, so broader use should be checked against experiments and other joint designs.
paper_title: Classification of welding-induced residual stresses in gusset joints
